input_path: data\papers\Heithoff et al. - A Digital Shadow for Accurate Robot Motion Control Integrating Data with Friction Models.pdf

extracted_characteristics.tooling_and_enablers: Digital Twin development utilizes Model‑Based Systems Engineering (MBSE) practices. Core modeling languages comprise SysML (v1.7) for functional and logical architecture, and architecture description languages (ADLs) such as ACME, AADL, and MontiArc. Behavioral aspects are represented by MontiArc Automaton and SysML state machines, while executable simulation models are constructed with MathWorks Simulink. The system supports code generation and integration in general‑purpose languages like C++ and Java. MBSE methodologies—including RFLP (Requirements, Functions, Logics, Physics), OOSEM, SPES, and SMArDT—are employed to manage multi‑level abstraction, handle stakeholder diversity, and structure the modeling workflow.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_time_scale: The Digital Twin operates in real‑time, ingesting live measurement streams from the physical asset and representing them as time‑series data. Synchronization between physical and digital states occurs at a frequency of up to 10 Hz (≈100 ms intervals), with an acceptable latency lower than 50 ms to preserve state fidelity. Temporal granularity is defined at the millisecond level, supporting high‑frequency sensor events and enabling time‑resolved simulation updates.
extracted_characteristics.dt_constellation: The Digital Twin system follows a modular architecture pattern driven by the Digital Shadow Reference Model (DSRM). Components include data trace collection, aggregation modules, model execution engines, and passive digital shadow adapters. The DSRM orchestrates these components in a pipeline that processes raw sensor logs into aggregated time‑series, applies simulation or predictive models, and updates the digital shadow. Resource management is handled by a central scheduler that assigns simulation workloads to available compute nodes, ensuring deterministic execution across the constellation.
extracted_characteristics.horizontal_integration: Integration with external enterprise systems is achieved via Asset Administration Shell (AAS) interfaces and MQTT publish/subscribe messaging. The digital twin exposes its data as AAS composition objects, enabling interoperability with PLM and MES systems. Sensor data streams are transmitted over MQTT with QoS level 1 to guarantee at least once delivery. The integration pattern follows a publish‑subscribe coupling, allowing other digital twins or cloud services to subscribe to relevant topics such as 'robot/axis/measurement'.
extracted_characteristics.dt_models_and_data: DT computational models encompass multi‑layered MBSE artifacts that are constructed with SysML Interaction Block Diagrams (IBDs) at the logical architecture level, MontiArc Automaton state‑machine models at the functional level, and SysML v1.7 state‑machine diagrams for detailed behavioral modeling. These models are often connected through architecture description languages (ADLs) such as ACME or AADL, enabling component‑and‑connector descriptions and cross‑notation synchronization. Continuous‑time behavior is represented with MathWorks Simulink models, while discrete‑time or real‑time execution is realized with C++ or Java code fragments. The DT constellation therefore integrates geometric, behavioral, and analytical representations (although the document only explicitly describes behavioral models) and relies on a one‑way data ingestion pipeline from the underlying physical system as defined in the Digital Shadow reference model. No explicit data‑management or aggregation mechanisms are detailed in the document.
extracted_characteristics.physical_to_virtual_interaction: Data from all sensors on the physical asset are collected as timestamped time‑series streams and transmitted to the digital shadow via an MQTT publish/subscribe mechanism. Each sensor measurement, consisting of a unique identifier, a numeric value, and an event timestamp, is published to a dedicated MQTT topic. The digital shadow receives these events, aggregates the raw sensor traces, and constructs higher‑level contextual abstractions that are stored for the defined monitoring purpose.
extracted_characteristics.dt_technical_connection: The communication backbone relies on the MQTT messaging protocol. Sensor data are conveyed through MQTT publish/subscribe exchanges, with each sensor linked to a designated topic. The MQTT broker handles message routing between physical data sources and the digital shadow. No other protocols or network topologies are defined in the document.
extracted_characteristics.life_cycle_stages: The Digital Twin lifecycle is organized according to the Model‑Based Systems Engineering (MBSE) abstraction hierarchy. 1) Requirements level – the physical system’s needs are captured with SysML requirement diagrams. 2) Functional architecture level – the system’s functions are decomposed with SysML IBDs or with architecture description languages such as ACME or AADL. 3) Logical architecture level – the functional blocks are refined into logical components, often described with state machines (SysML v1.7) or MontiArc automata, and connected via the chosen ADL. 4) Product/technical level – the logical model is instantiated into executable artefacts, e.g., Simulink simulation models, generated C++ or Java code, and finally integrated with the physical system. The document does not describe explicit lifecycle governance mechanisms such as version control or configuration management, nor detailed transition triggers between stages.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_process_and_dt_evolution: Digital Twin creation follows established MBSE methods – RFLP (Requirements, Functions, Logics, Physics), OOSEM, SPES and SMArDT – and employs architecture description languages such as ACME, AADL, MontiArc and SysML IBDs to capture component and connector information. System behavior is modeled with state machines (SysML v1.7) or MontiArc automata, and dynamic aspects are simulated with MathWorks Simulink. Semantic context is encapsulated in code written in C++ or Java. The provided documents do not detail quality‑assurance procedures, formal testing, or version‑control practices for the Twin models.

II. B ACKGROUND

To better understand our approach, we describe relevant
modeling methods, important concepts for DSs, and methods
on how to derive concrete DSs.


_A. Modeling Software and Systems_

To describe the structure and behavior of systems, ModelBased Systems-Engineering (MBSE) uses systems engineering
modeling languages such as SysML [17] during the development phase. Due to the complexity of systems, modeling
is typically split into different levels of abstraction, e.g.,
requirements, functional architecture, logical architecture, and
product/technical layer. There exist different methodologies
on how to handle this complexity, e.g., RFLP (Requirements, Functions, Logics, Physics) [18], [19], OOSEM [20],
SPES [21], or SMArDT [22]. To describe the functional
and logical architecture, typically architecture description languages (ADLs) are used. As main concepts, they describe
systems from a component & connector perspective, e.g.,
ACME [23], AADL [24], MontiArc [25], [26] or SysML
IBDs [17], [27]. These typically describe the system’s components, their interfaces, their interconnections, and composition.
Some ADLs also include methods to describe the system’s
behavior, e.g., MontiArc Automaton or state machines in
SysML v1.7. To describe behavior in more detail, these models
are often combined with further (modeling) tools such as
MathWorks Simulink [28], other simulation tools and methods [29], [30], or code in general-purpose languages like C++
or Java.

Thus, a comprehensive description of a system from an
MBSE perspective might have to cope with models in different
modeling notations and different tools. In addition, various
heterogeneous stakeholders and perspectives need to be integrated [31].


_B. Digital Shadow Reference Model_

Kritzinger et al. [32] differentiate digital shadows from
digital twins based on their communication flow. A digital
shadow is characterized by “ _an automated one-way data_
_flow between the state of an existing physical object and_
_a digital object_ [32]”. In practical terms, this means that a
digital shadow functions as a passive entity: it neither operates
autonomously nor pushes data back to the physical system.
Therefore, software systems require active components to
interpret and process this incoming data [33], [34]. A Digital
Twin (DT) can provide such active components [35]–[37].
In the remainder of this paper, we understand a digital
shadow as _“a set of contextual data traces and their ag-_
_gregation and abstraction collected for a specific purpose_



**Fig. 1:** Digital shadow reference model as a UML class
diagram. It defines important concepts for digital shadows in
manufacturing.

block_1_judge.reasoning: The document explicitly identifies the physical system as the 'MABI MAX 100' industrial robot, describing its structure, components, and operational domain. Claiming this is 'Not in Document' is incorrect. Quote: 'The focus of this paper is the serial robot MABI MAX 100 with six axes from the Swiss company MABI Robotic AG'

The document describes services provided by the digital shadow, such as visualization of data and friction identification to improve control accuracy. Claiming this is 'Not in Document' is incorrect. Quote: 'live-data linking as well as visualization in a dashboard' and 'enable the continuous, multi-physics-aware identification of joint friction'

The extraction is highly detailed and accurately reflects the comprehensive list of tools, languages, and methodologies described in the 'Background' section. It correctly identifies MBSE practices, specific modeling languages (SysML, ACME, AADL, MontiArc), simulation tools (Simulink), programming languages (C++, Java), and specific methodologies (RFLP, OOSEM, SPES, SMArDT).

block_2_judge.reasoning: The extraction claims specific synchronization frequencies (10 Hz), latency lower than 50 ms, and millisecond granularity which are not found in the source text. While the text mentions 'real-time, data-driven representations' and 'live measurements', it does not provide the specific temporal metrics cited in the extraction.

The document describes a single digital shadow for a specific robot axis ('FrictionShadow acts as the identification element for one digital shadow'), and does not discuss multiple instances, decentralized architectures, or coordination between twins.

The extraction correctly identifies the Digital Shadow Reference Model (DSRM) but adds unsupported details such as a 'central scheduler', 'compute nodes', and 'passive digital shadow adapters'. The text describes DSRM concepts like the 'Processing component' and 'Simulation models' but does not describe the orchestration infrastructure or resource management claimed.

The extraction correctly identifies the use of Asset Administration Shells (AAS) and MQTT. However, it fabricates technical details such as 'AAS composition objects', interoperability with 'PLM and MES', 'QoS level 1', and specific topic strings ('robot/axis/measurement'), none of which appear in the source text.

block_5_judge.reasoning: The extraction perfectly aligns with Section II.A, which details the MBSE abstraction hierarchy. It accurately lists the four levels (requirements, functional architecture, logical architecture, and product/technical layer) and the corresponding modeling notations and tools (SysML, Simulink, C++/Java). It correctly notes the lack of governance details. Quotes: 'modeling is typically split into different levels of abstraction, e.g., requirements, functional architecture, logical architecture, and product/technical layer', 'To describe the functional and logical architecture, typically architecture description languages (ADLs) are used... ACME, AADL, MontiArc', 'often combined with further (modeling) tools such as MathWorks Simulink... or code in general-purpose languages like C++ or Java'.

The extraction accurately summarizes the engineering processes described in Section II.A and II.C. It correctly identifies the MBSE methodologies (RFLP, OOSEM, SPES, SMArDT) and the architecture description languages (ACME, AADL, MontiArc, SysML IBDs) used. It also correctly captures the mention of behavior modeling tools (Simulink, state machines) and the absence of specific quality assurance procedures. Quotes: 'methodologies on how to handle this complexity, e.g., RFLP (Requirements, Functions, Logics, Physics)... OOSEM... SPES... or SMArDT', 'MathWorks Simulink... or code in general-purpose languages like C++ or Java', 'To define digital shadows requires collecting all relevant information... The main steps are: 1) Problem identification...'.

The extraction states 'Not in Document', but the source documents explicitly mention user interaction methods and the types of insights generated. The abstract and Section IV describe a dashboard for visualization and the detection of friction parameters. Quotes: 'live-data linking as well as visualization in a dashboard', 'display it in a dashboard', 'continuous, multi-physics-aware identification of joint friction'. Since evidence exists, the claim is incorrect.

The extraction states 'Not in Document'. While the source mentions standard modeling languages (SysML, UML) and Asset Administration Shells (AAS), it does not discuss 'standards compliance,' 'certification requirements,' 'compliance frameworks,' or specific 'interoperability standards adopted' in the manner requested by the characteristic description. Thus, the assessment that relevant standardization details are not present is correct.
